items.title: Early Recognition and Treatment of Urinary Tract Obstruction (UTO)
items.content: Prompt identification and management of UTO are crucial to prevent or reverse kidney damage, reduce the risk of hypertension, infection, and stone formation. Chronic obstruction can lead to permanent renal atrophy.
keyPoints: Obstruction leads to stasis and elevated urinary tract pressure, impairing renal function.
Early treatment can prevent acute and chronic kidney disease (obstructive nephropathy).
Chronic UTO may result in permanent loss of renal mass and excretory capability.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.title: Genetic Causes and Implications of Lower Urinary Tract Obstruction (LUTO)
items.content: Recent studies have identified genetic mutations linked to CAKUT, which can lead to low nephron number. Fetal LUTO untreated may cause pulmonary hypoplasia or kidney failure later in life.
keyPoints: Genetic mutations implicated in CAKUT include duplications/deletions and genes like HFN1β, PAX2, SIX1, GATA3.
Obstructive disease in adulthood can originate from decreased nephron number during fetal development.
Fetal LUTO may result in pulmonary hypoplasia or kidney failure; oligohydramnios/anhydramnios are diagnostic clues.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.title: Common Sites and Causes of Urinary Tract Obstruction
items.content: Urinary tract obstruction can occur at any level from renal tubules to the external urethral meatus. Common sites include the ureteropelvic and ureterovesical junctions, bladder neck, and urethral meatus. In children, congenital malformations like UPJ narrowing are prevalent, while in adults, acquired defects such as pelvic tumors and calculi are more common.
keyPoints: Obstruction can occur at any level of the urinary tract.
Common sites include ureteropelvic and ureterovesical junctions, bladder neck, and urethral meatus.
Childhood causes: congenital malformations (e.g., UPJ narrowing).
Adult causes: pelvic tumors, calculi, urethral stricture.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Functional Impairment and Secondary Contributors to Obstruction
items.content: Functional impairment of urine flow can result from abnormal micturition control due to neurogenic bladder or medications. Conditions like diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and metabolic disturbances can exacerbate chronic kidney disease resulting from obstruction.
keyPoints: Functional impairment may be asymptomatic or present with lower urinary tract symptoms.
Causes include neurogenic bladder, α-adrenergic and anticholinergic agents, opiates.
Secondary contributors to chronic kidney disease: diabetes mellitus, hypertension.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Diagnostic Tools for Anatomic Obstruction
items.content: Urinary flow measurements and postvoid residual measurement are key diagnostic tools. Bladder volume can be assessed by bedside ultrasound, while cystourethroscopy and urodynamic studies evaluate bladder function in symptomatic patients.
keyPoints: Diagnose anatomic obstruction with urinary flow measurements and postvoid residual measurement.
Bedside ultrasound assesses bladder volume.
Cystourethroscopy and urodynamic studies for evaluating bladder function.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.title: Understanding Flank Pain in UTO
items.content: Flank pain is a primary symptom leading to medical attention for urinary tract obstruction (UTO). It results from distention of the collecting system or renal capsule, with severity influenced by the rate of distention rather than its degree. Acute supravesical obstructions cause severe, intermittent pain known as renal colic, which can radiate to lower abdomen, testes, or labia.
keyPoints: Flank pain leads to medical attention for UTO.
Pain severity is influenced by the rate of distention.
Acute supravesical obstruction causes severe, intermittent renal colic.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Azotemia and Renal Failure in UTO
items.content: Azotemia develops when excretory function is impaired due to obstruction, often seen with bladder outlet or bilateral renal pelvic/ureteric obstruction. Complete bilateral obstruction should be suspected if acute renal failure is accompanied by anuria. Patients with unexplained renal failure or risk factors like nephrolithiasis and prostatic enlargement should be evaluated for UTO.
keyPoints: Azotemia occurs when excretory function is impaired due to obstruction.
Complete bilateral obstruction suspected with acute renal failure and anuria.
Evaluate patients with unexplained renal failure or risk factors for UTO.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Tubular Dysfunction in Partial Bilateral UTO
items.content: Partial bilateral UTO leads to acquired distal renal tubular acidosis, hyperkalemia, and renal salt wasting. Dysregulation of aquaporin-2 water channels contributes to polyuria, resembling nephrogenic diabetes insipidus. The $ H^{+} $-ATPase in collecting duct cells is disrupted, impairing $ H^{+} $ secretion and contributing to metabolic acidosis.
keyPoints: Partial bilateral UTO causes distal renal tubular acidosis, hyperkalemia, and salt wasting.
Dysregulation of aquaporin-2 leads to polyuria.
$ H^{+} $-ATPase disruption impairs $ H^{+} $ secretion.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Recognize UTO in UTI or urolithiasis due to risk of complications
items.content: Urinary tract obstruction (UTO) should be considered in patients presenting with urinary tract infections or urolithiasis, as these conditions can lead to urinary stasis and promote bacterial growth. Hypertension is a frequent consequence of acute and subacute unilateral UTO due to increased renin release by the affected kidney.
keyPoints: UTO must be considered in patients with UTIs or urolithiasis.
Hypertension is common in acute/subacute unilateral obstruction from increased renin release.
Chronic bilateral UTO can lead to significant hypertension due to extracellular volume expansion.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Use ultrasonography for initial evaluation of suspected UTO
items.content: Ultrasonography is a key diagnostic tool for evaluating renal and bladder size, as well as pyelocalyceal contour in cases of suspected urinary tract obstruction. It has approximately 90% specificity and sensitivity for detecting hydronephrosis.
keyPoints: Abdominal ultrasonography evaluates renal and bladder size, and pyelocalyceal contour.
~90% specific and sensitive for detection of hydronephrosis.
False positives may occur with diuresis, renal cysts, or normal congenital variants.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Utilize noncontrast CT for acute flank pain and stone characterization
items.content: Noncontrast computed tomography (CT) is the preferred imaging modality for evaluating acute flank pain, as it can identify both intrinsic and extrinsic sites of obstruction. It also allows differentiation between types of renal stones based on Hounsfield units.
keyPoints: Noncontrast CT is the test of choice for acute flank pain evaluation.
CT identifies intrinsic and extrinsic sites of obstruction.
Differentiates stone types by measuring radiodensity in Hounsfield units.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Immediate Intervention for UTO with Infection
items.content: Urinary tract obstruction (UTO) complicated by infection requires urgent relief to prevent generalized sepsis and renal damage. Prompt urologic intervention is necessary, often involving drainage via nephrostomy, ureterostomy, or catheterization.
keyPoints: Immediate relief of UTO with infection is crucial to prevent sepsis and renal damage.
Drainage methods include nephrostomy, ureterostomy, or catheterization (ureteral, urethral, suprapubic).
Prolonged antibiotic treatment may be necessary.
Nephrectomy might be needed for chronic/recurrent infections in poorly functioning obstructed kidneys.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · TREATMENT | Urinary Tract Obstruction | PROGNOSIS

items.title: Prognosis and Timing of Obstruction Relief
items.content: The prognosis for renal function recovery after UTO relief depends on whether irreversible damage has occurred. Complete obstruction with infection can destroy the kidney within days, while partial return of glomerular filtration rate may follow relief of complete obstruction lasting 1-2 weeks; however, recovery is unlikely after 8 weeks.
keyPoints: Renal function prognosis post-obstruction relief depends on irreversible damage presence.
Complete obstruction with infection can lead to kidney destruction within days.
Partial return of glomerular filtration rate possible after 1-2 weeks of complete obstruction relief.
Recovery unlikely after 8 weeks of obstruction.
Renal radionuclide scan post-decompression may predict renal dysfunction reversibility.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · TREATMENT | Urinary Tract Obstruction | PROGNOSIS

items.title: Understanding Postobstructive Diuresis Mechanisms
items.content: Postobstructive diuresis occurs after relief of bilateral complete urinary obstruction, leading to polyuria due to the excretion of retained urea and electrolyte-free water. This is driven by increased natriuretic factors, correction of extracellular volume expansion, and decreased salt and water reabsorption.
keyPoints: Polyuria occurs after relief of bilateral complete urinary obstruction.
Urine is hypotonic with high sodium chloride, potassium, phosphate, and magnesium.
Natriuresis results from correction of extracellular volume expansion and increased natriuretic factors.
Electrolyte-free water excretion is indicated when the sum of urinary sodium and potassium concentrations is lower than serum sodium concentration.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · POSTOBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS

items.title: Management of Postobstructive Diuresis
items.content: Management involves monitoring urinary output and serum/urine electrolyte concentrations to guide intravenous fluid replacement. Aggressive fluid management may be necessary in cases of hypovolemia or electrolyte disturbances, often requiring 0.45% saline to match urine electrolyte concentrations.
keyPoints: Monitor urinary output and serum/urine sodium, potassium, and osmolal concentrations.
Intravenous fluids should replace less than urinary losses to prevent complications.
Aggressive fluid management is needed in hypovolemia or electrolyte disturbances.
0.45% saline may be required for appropriate replacement of urine electrolytes.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · POSTOBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS

items.question: Which of the following conditions can chronic urinary tract obstruction (UTO) lead to if not promptly treated?
options: Permanent loss of renal mass and excretory capability
items.explanation: Chronic obstruction may lead to permanent loss of renal mass (renal atrophy) and excretory capability.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.question: What is a common cause of acute and chronic kidney disease related to urinary tract issues?
items.explanation: Obstruction to the flow of urine, with attendant stasis and elevation in urinary tract pressure, impairs renal functions and is a common cause of acute and chronic kidney disease (obstructive nephropathy).
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.question: What recent developments have advanced our understanding of lower urinary tract obstruction (LUTO) and CAKUT?
options: Studies in mice, zebrafish, and kidney organoids identifying genetic mutations
New surgical techniques for relieving obstruction
Development of new antibiotics targeting infections
items.explanation: Recent developments include studies in mice, zebrafish, and pluripotent stem cells (kidney organoids) identifying possible genetic mutations implicated in CAKUT.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.question: Which of the following is a common site for mechanical obstruction within the urinary tract?
items.explanation: The ureteropelvic junction is a normal point of narrowing and a common site for obstruction.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.question: What condition may result from severe vesicoureteral reflux in children?
items.explanation: Severe vesicoureteral reflux can lead to recurrent urinary infections, hypertension, and renal scarring in childhood.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which is the most common cause of bilateral hydronephrosis in boys?
items.explanation: Posterior urethral valves are the most common cause of bilateral hydronephrosis in boys.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.question: What is a potential consequence of α-adrenergic and anticholinergic agents on urine flow?
items.explanation: α-Adrenergic and anticholinergic agents can lead to urinary retention.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.question: Which symptom is pathognomonic of vesicoureteral reflux?
options: Flank pain that occurs only with micturition
items.explanation: Flank pain occurring only with micturition is specifically indicative of vesicoureteral reflux.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What contributes to the excruciating, intermittent pain known as renal colic?
options: Distention of the collecting system or renal capsule
Increased hydrostatic pressures proximal to the site of obstruction
Acute supravesical obstruction from a stone lodged in a ureter
items.explanation: Renal colic is associated with acute supravesical obstruction, such as a stone in the ureter.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What condition should be suspected when acute renal failure is accompanied by anuria?
items.explanation: Anuria with acute renal failure suggests complete bilateral obstruction.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a consequence of partial bilateral UTO?
options: Decreased salt reabsorption in proximal tubule
Hypernatremia due to excessive fluid intake
items.explanation: Partial bilateral UTO leads to downregulation of salt reabsorption in the proximal tubule.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following complications can arise from obstructive uropathy due to increased erythropoietin production?
items.explanation: Erythrocytosis is an infrequent complication of obstructive uropathy secondary to increased erythropoietin production.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What imaging modality is considered the test of choice for acute flank pain due to its ability to measure the radiodensity of renal stones?
items.explanation: Noncontrast CT scan is the test of choice for acute flank pain as it measures the radiodensity of renal stones in Hounsfield units.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition may be mistaken for renal cystic disease on ultrasonography?
items.explanation: Congenital UPJ obstruction may be mistaken for renal cystic disease on ultrasonography.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a potential limitation of abdominal ultrasonography in detecting hydronephrosis?
options: False-positive results with diuresis or renal cysts
items.explanation: Abdominal ultrasonography may give false-positive results for hydronephrosis due to diuresis, renal cysts, or an extrarenal pelvis.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which intervention is necessary when urinary tract obstruction (UTO) is complicated by infection?
options: Immediate relief of the obstruction to prevent sepsis and renal damage.
Delaying surgery until acid-base, fluid, and electrolyte status are restored.
Use of $ \alpha $-adrenergic blockers for immediate treatment.
Initiation of nephrectomy as a first-line treatment.
items.explanation: Immediate relief is necessary to prevent sepsis and renal damage when UTO is complicated by infection.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · TREATMENT | Urinary Tract Obstruction | PROGNOSIS

items.question: What treatment approach is recommended for benign prostatic hypertrophy in the context of urinary tract obstruction?
items.explanation: Benign prostatic hypertrophy may be treated with $ \alpha $-adrenergic blockers and $ \delta $-reductase inhibitors.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · TREATMENT | Urinary Tract Obstruction | PROGNOSIS

items.question: What is a potential risk associated with cataract surgery in patients taking $ \alpha $-adrenergic blockers?
items.explanation: There is a risk of intraoperative floppy iris syndrome in patients taking $ \alpha $-adrenergic blockers during cataract surgery.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · TREATMENT | Urinary Tract Obstruction | PROGNOSIS

items.question: Which condition is commonly associated with the relief of bilateral complete urinary tract obstruction?
options: Hypertension due to increased renal perfusion
Acute kidney injury from reperfusion injury
items.explanation: Relief of bilateral complete obstruction commonly results in polyuria, which may be massive.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · POSTOBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS

items.question: What is a recognized indicator of electrolyte-free water excretion (hypotonic urine) in postobstructive diuresis?
options: The sum of urinary sodium and potassium concentrations exceeds serum sodium concentration.
The sum of urinary sodium and potassium concentrations equals serum sodium concentration.
The sum of urinary sodium and potassium concentrations is lower than the serum sodium concentration.
Urinary osmolality is higher than plasma osmolality.
items.explanation: Electrolyte-free water excretion (hypotonic urine) is recognized when the sum of urinary sodium and potassium concentrations is lower than the serum sodium concentration.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · POSTOBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS

items.question: What can precipitate postobstructive diuresis?
options: Suppression of antidiuretic hormone at arterial baroreceptor sites
items.explanation: Precipitating factors include suppression of antidiuretic hormone at arterial baroreceptor sites.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · POSTOBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS

items.statement: Obstructive nephropathy can lead to hypertension.
items.explanation: UTO increases susceptibility to hypertension due to elevated urinary tract pressure.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.statement: There is ongoing nephrogenesis in humans after birth.
items.explanation: There is no nephrogenesis in humans after birth; obstructive disease manifesting later may originate from decreased nephron number during fetal development.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.statement: Functional impairment of urine flow is always associated with mechanical obstruction.
items.explanation: Functional impairment can occur without fixed occlusion, due to altered voiding from abnormal micturition control.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Hydronephrosis in pregnancy is more common on the left side.
items.explanation: Hydronephrosis during pregnancy occurs more often on the right side due to ureteral compression by the enlarged uterus.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.statement: Increased prostaglandin E₂ contributes to increased salt reabsorption along the nephron.
items.explanation: Prostaglandin E₂ actually contributes to decreased salt reabsorption.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Azotemia with hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis should prompt consideration of UTO.
items.explanation: These symptoms are indicative of urinary tract obstruction.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Hydronephrosis may be absent on ultrasound when obstruction is less than 48 hours in duration.
items.explanation: Hydronephrosis can be absent on ultrasound if the obstruction has been present for less than 48 hours.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Magnetic resonance urography is currently superior to CT scan in evaluating UTO.
items.explanation: Magnetic resonance urography is not at this time considered superior to the CT scan for evaluating urinary tract obstruction.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Complete obstruction with infection can lead to total destruction of the kidney within days.
items.explanation: Complete obstruction with infection can indeed cause rapid and severe renal damage.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · TREATMENT | Urinary Tract Obstruction | PROGNOSIS

items.statement: Partial return of glomerular filtration rate is likely after 8 weeks of complete obstruction.
items.explanation: Recovery of renal function is unlikely after 8 weeks of complete obstruction.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · TREATMENT | Urinary Tract Obstruction | PROGNOSIS

items.statement: In postobstructive diuresis, the urine is typically hypertonic.
items.explanation: The urine in postobstructive diuresis is usually hypotonic.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · POSTOBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS

items.statement: Replacement with intravenous fluids less than urinary losses can prevent complications of postobstructive diuresis.
items.explanation: Replacement with intravenous fluids in amounts less than urinary losses usually prevents iatrogenic expansion of extracellular volume.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · POSTOBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS

items.assertion: Obstructive nephropathy can result in chronic kidney failure.
items.reason: Chronic obstruction leads to renal atrophy and loss of excretory capability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · Urinary Tract Obstruction

items.assertion: Vesicoureteral reflux in children often resolves with age.
items.reason: Vesicoureteral reflux is frequently due to transient developmental abnormalities that improve over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why vesicoureteral reflux often resolves.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Benign prostatic hypertrophy always causes bladder outlet obstruction.
items.reason: Lower urinary tract symptoms in men over 40 years are associated with benign prostatic hypertrophy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; lower urinary tract symptoms may occur without actual bladder outlet obstruction.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: Increased hydrostatic pressures proximal to the site of obstruction lead to pain.
items.reason: The buildup of pressure results in distention of the collecting system and elevated intratubular pressures that initiate tubular dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Dysregulation of aquaporin-2 water channels improves with administration of vasopressin.
items.reason: This defect is a form of acquired nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the defect does not improve with vasopressin.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 1)

items.assertion: Noncontrast CT scans are safer for patients at risk for contrast nephropathy.
items.reason: Noncontrast CT scans do not require the use of iodinated contrast agents, which can cause contrast-induced acute kidney injury.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why noncontrast CT scans are safer for patients at risk for contrast nephropathy.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Duplex Doppler ultrasonography can detect an increased resistive index in urinary obstruction.
items.reason: An increased resistive index is indicative of decreased blood flow resistance, which occurs with obstruction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not correctly explain why duplex Doppler ultrasonography detects an increased resistive index; it actually indicates increased resistance.
items.reference: Urinary Tract Obstruction · CLINICAL FEATURES AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS
